subject: English
title: Grammar and Structure
outline: Kinds of nouns and their functions in sentences
Personal, possessive, reflexive and relative pronouns
Simple present, past and future tenses

modules.title: Nouns — classification, gender, number and function
modules.html:
A noun is a word that names a person, place, thing, quality or idea. Nouns are the naming words of English, and every sentence usually contains at least one.

1. Classification

  • Proper noun — a specific name; always capitalised: Lagos, Chinua Achebe, Monday.
  • Common noun — a general name: city, writer, day.
  • Concrete noun — something perceivable by the senses: book, rain, laughter.
  • Abstract noun — an idea, feeling or quality: honesty, freedom, patience.
  • Collective noun — a group taken as a unit: team, herd, jury, flock.
  • Countable vs uncountable: chair/chairs (countable); water, information, furniture (uncountable — no plural, no a/an).

2. Gender

English recognises four genders:

  • Masculine: king, actor, lion.
  • Feminine: queen, actress, lioness.
  • Common (either sex): student, doctor, teacher, cousin.
  • Neuter (no sex): book, chair, honesty.

3. Number — forming plurals

  • Most nouns add -s: cat → cats.
  • Nouns ending in -s, -x, -ch, -sh add -es: bus → buses, box → boxes.
  • Consonant + -y changes to -ies: baby → babies; but vowel + -y just adds -s: boy → boys.
  • Some -f/-fe nouns change to -ves: leaf → leaves, knife → knives. Exceptions: roof → roofs, chief → chiefs.
  • Irregular plurals: man → men, foot → feet, child → children, mouse → mice, ox → oxen, criterion → criteria.
  • Unchanging: sheep, deer, aircraft, series.

4. Functions of the noun in a sentence

  1. Subject — The teacher arrived.
  2. Object of a verb — She read the book.
  3. Object of a preposition — He sat on the chair.
  4. Complement — Ada became a doctor.
  5. Apposition — My brother, Musa, is a pilot.

examples.q: Give the plural of ox, sheep, criterion.
examples.a: oxen, sheep, criteria. Sheep is unchanging; criterion takes its Greek plural.

examples.q: Underline the noun and state its function: Freedom is precious to every citizen.
examples.a: Freedom — subject of the verb is. It is an abstract noun.

modules.title: Pronouns — personal, relative, possessive, reflexive
modules.html:
A pronoun replaces a noun so we do not repeat it. The noun it stands for is called its antecedent.

1. Personal pronouns

PersonSubjectObject
1st sg / plI / weme / us
2ndyouyou
3rd sghe, she, ithim, her, it
3rd pltheythem

Subject pronouns act as the doer; object pronouns receive the action: She saw him at the market.

2. Possessive pronouns and adjectives

  • Possessive adjective (before a noun): my, your, his, her, its, our, their — my book.
  • Possessive pronoun (standing alone): mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs — That book is mine.
  • Note: its = belonging to it (no apostrophe); it's = it is.

3. Reflexive pronouns

Formed with -self / -selves: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves. Use them when the subject and object are the same person — She hurt herself — or for emphasis — I built it myself. Never say hisself or theirselves.

4. Relative pronouns

  • who / whom — for people (who as subject, whom as object).
  • whose — possession.
  • which — for things and animals.
  • that — people or things (defining clauses only).

The girl who won the prize is my sister. — who refers back to girl.

examples.q: Correct: Between you and (I / me), the plan is risky.
examples.a: me — after the preposition between, we need the object form.

examples.q: Fill in with a relative pronoun: The boy _____ bicycle was stolen has reported to the police.

modules.title: Verbs — finite / non-finite, auxiliaries, simple tenses, voice
modules.html:
A verb expresses action (write, run) or a state (be, seem). Every complete sentence must have at least one finite verb.

1. Finite vs non-finite

  • Finite verbs change their form to agree with the subject and to show tense: She writes. They wrote.
  • Non-finite verbs do not change with the subject. Three types:
    • Infinitive: to write.
    • Gerund (-ing, acts as a noun): Writing is fun.
    • Participle: present (writing) and past (written), used in continuous / perfect tenses and as adjectives.

2. Auxiliary (helping) verbs

  • Primary: be, have, do — help form tenses, questions and negatives.
  • Modal: can, could, may, might, must, shall, should, will, would, ought to — express ability, permission, obligation, possibility.

3. The three simple tenses

TenseFormExample
Simple presentbase (+ -s for 3rd sg)She writes daily.
Simple pastbase + -ed / irregularShe wrote yesterday.
Simple futurewill / shall + baseShe will write tomorrow.

4. Active vs passive voice

In the active voice the subject does the action; in the passive the subject receives it.

  • Active: The teacher marked the scripts.
  • Passive: The scripts were marked by the teacher.

Formula: form of be + past participle. The doer, if mentioned, follows by.

examples.q: Change to the passive: Musa is reading the novel.
examples.a: The novel is being read by Musa. — present continuous passive uses is being + past participle.

examples.q: Identify the non-finite verb in: She loves to sing in the choir.
examples.a: to sing — an infinitive. The finite verb is loves.

modules.title: Adjectives — order, degrees of comparison
modules.html:
An adjective describes or modifies a noun or pronoun: a bright student, the sky is blue.

1. Order of adjectives

When several adjectives modify one noun, English arranges them in a fixed order — OSASCOMP: Opinion → Size → Age → Shape → Colour → Origin → Material → Purpose.

Example: a lovely small old round brown Nigerian wooden dining table.

2. Degrees of comparison

  • Positive: tall, beautiful, good.
  • Comparative: taller, more beautiful, better.
  • Superlative: tallest, most beautiful, best.

Rules of thumb:

  • 1-syllable adjectives add -er / -est: fast → faster → fastest.
  • 2-syllable ending in -y change -y to -ier / -iest: happy → happier → happiest.
  • Adjectives of 3+ syllables use more / most: beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful.
  • Irregular: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst; little → less → least; many/much → more → most.

3. Punctuation and capitalisation reminders

  • Capitalise the first word of a sentence, proper nouns, months, days, and titles used with names.
  • Use a comma before and / but / or when joining two independent clauses.
  • Semicolons join two closely related independent clauses: Ada was tired; she slept early.
  • An apostrophe shows possession (Musa's book) or contraction (don't).

examples.q: Arrange correctly: brown / small / leather / two / old / bags.
examples.a: two small old brown leather bags — number → size → age → colour → material.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an abstract noun?
quiz.solution: Honesty is a quality — you cannot see or touch it — so it is abstract.

quiz.q: The plural of criterion is:
quiz.solution: Words from Greek ending in -on take -a in the plural: criterion → criteria.

quiz.q: Choose the correct sentence.
options: Between you and I, he is wrong.
Between you and me, he is wrong.
Between I and you, he is wrong.
Between me and you, he is wrong.
quiz.solution: After the preposition between we need the object form me, and courtesy places the other person first.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses who correctly?
options: The girl whom won the race is my sister.
The girl who I saw is my sister.
The girl who won the race is my sister.
The girl whose won the race is my sister.
quiz.solution: Who is used because it is the subject of the verb won inside the relative clause.

quiz.q: The word team is a…
quiz.solution: A team is a group of players viewed as one unit — a collective noun.

quiz.q: Which of the following is uncountable?
quiz.solution: Information is uncountable — no plural, no an.

quiz.q: Feminine gender of bachelor:
quiz.solution: Bachelor / spinster is the traditional pair for an unmarried man / woman.

quiz.q: Choose the plural of leaf.
quiz.solution: Most -f/-fe nouns change to -ves: leaf → leaves.

quiz.q: Identify the reflexive pronoun.
quiz.solution: Reflexive pronouns end in -self / -selves.

quiz.q: Which word is a possessive adjective?
quiz.solution: My comes before a noun (my book); mine stands alone.

quiz.q: The bell in the school bell is a…
quiz.solution: Bell is still a noun; school is used attributively (like an adjective) but bell is the head noun.

quiz.q: Choose the correct verb: Neither the boys nor the girl _____ ready.
quiz.solution: With neither…nor, the verb agrees with the nearer subject — the girl is singular, so is.

quiz.q: Which verb form is non-finite?
quiz.solution: Writing (participle / gerund) does not change with the subject, so it is non-finite.

quiz.q: Change to passive: They built the house last year.
options: The house is built last year.
The house was built last year.
The house has been built last year.
The house had been built last year.
quiz.solution: Simple past passive = was/were + past participle.

quiz.q: Which sentence is in the active voice?
options: The letter was written by Ada.
quiz.solution: In (c) the subject chairman performs the action.

quiz.q: Comparative form of little (meaning amount):
quiz.solution: Little → less → least for uncountable amounts.

quiz.q: The superlative of good is:
quiz.solution: Good → better → best is irregular.

quiz.q: Arrange in correct order: Nigerian / small / old / wooden chair.
quiz.solution: Order = Size → Age → Origin → Material.

quiz.q: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
options: Ada, Musa and Sade are friends.
Ada Musa and Sade are friends.
Ada Musa, and Sade, are friends.
Ada, Musa and, Sade are friends.
quiz.solution: Commas separate items in a series; the final comma before and is optional in British style.

quiz.q: Which noun is common gender?
quiz.solution: Cousin can be male or female — common gender.

quiz.q: Choose the correct plural: mouse (the animal).
quiz.solution: Irregular plural: mouse → mice.

quiz.q: Which is a proper noun?
quiz.solution: Niger is the specific name of a river — proper noun.

quiz.q: The children are playing. The tense is:
quiz.solution: are + playing = present continuous.

quiz.q: Auxiliary verb in She has finished her meal:
quiz.solution: Has helps form the present perfect.

quiz.q: Which is a modal auxiliary?
quiz.solution: Must, can, may, should… are modals.

quiz.q: Correct the sentence: He don't know the answer.
options: He does not knew the answer.
quiz.solution: 3rd person singular takes doesn't, not don't.

quiz.q: Choose the correct question tag: She sings beautifully, _____?
quiz.solution: Positive statement in simple present takes a negative tag with doesn't.

quiz.q: Fill in: The book _____ cover is torn belongs to me.
quiz.solution: Whose shows possession, even for things.

quiz.q: It is I who am to blame. — the underlined word is a:
quiz.solution: I is a 1st-person personal pronoun.

quiz.q: Choose the correct sentence.
options: Each of the boys have a book.
Each of the boys has a book.
Each of the boys are having a book.
Each of the boys were having a book.
quiz.solution: Each is singular, so takes has.

quiz.q: The word swimming in Swimming is fun is a:
quiz.solution: An -ing form acting as a noun is a gerund; here it is the subject.

quiz.q: Which sentence uses the apostrophe correctly?
quiz.solution: Boys here is a simple plural; no apostrophe.

quiz.q: Possessive of children:
quiz.solution: Irregular plurals not ending in -s add 's: children's.

quiz.q: Choose the reflexive form: I hurt _____ while cooking.
quiz.solution: Subject = object = I, so use the reflexive myself.

quiz.q: Which is a countable noun?
quiz.solution: Chair can be counted (one chair, two chairs).

quiz.q: Adverb form of quick:
quiz.solution: Add -ly to make the adverb.

quiz.q: The sentence Never have I seen such a mess begins with:
quiz.solution: Never is an adverb; fronting it inverts subject and auxiliary.

quiz.q: Simple past of lie (to recline):
quiz.solution: Lie / lay / lain. Lied is the past of lie = tell an untruth.

quiz.q: Simple past of lay (to place):
quiz.solution: Lay / laid / laid.

quiz.q: Choose the correct sentence.
options: The news have been bad today.
quiz.solution: News is singular and uncountable.

quiz.q: Which noun is always plural in form?
quiz.solution: Instruments in two parts — scissors, trousers, tongs — are always plural.

quiz.q: Passive of Somebody has stolen my bag.
options: My bag was stolen by somebody.
quiz.solution: Present perfect passive: has been + past participle. The agent somebody is dropped as unimportant.

quiz.q: Choose the sentence with correct capitalisation.
options: We travel to lagos every december.
We travel to Lagos every December.
We Travel to Lagos every December.
we travel to Lagos every december.
quiz.solution: Proper nouns (Lagos, December) are capitalised; common verbs are not.

quiz.q: Feminine of hero:
quiz.solution: Heroine — the feminine hero. Heroin is a drug.

quiz.q: Which word is an abstract noun?
quiz.solution: Freedom is an idea, not something you can touch.

quiz.q: Change to future tense: She writes a letter.
quiz.solution: Will/shall + base form.

quiz.q: Which sentence contains an adverb?
quiz.solution: Loudly modifies the verb sings.

quiz.q: The word that in The book that I bought is new is a:
quiz.solution: It introduces a relative clause modifying book.

quiz.q: Choose the correct sentence.
options: Neither of the answers are correct.
Neither of the answers is correct.
Neither of the answers were correct.
Neither of the answers have been correct.
quiz.solution: Neither takes a singular verb.

quiz.q: Underline the subject: Under the table sat a cat.
quiz.solution: Inverted order: the true subject is a cat.
